game_description: Build a wildlife preserve by attracting birds with unique abilities.

strategies.strategy_detailed: Pick bonus cards aligned with birds you're likely to draft. 'Counting' bonuses (X+ birds with a specific trait — wingspan range, name pattern, food type) score reliably when your engine matches them. Skip bonuses requiring rare combos or specific late-game triggers that may never fire.
strategy_stealth: Match bonus to your draft direction

strategies.strategy_detailed: Keep birds that cost 1-2 food total unless they have a powerful 'when played' or 'once between turns' ability. High-cost birds (4+ food) are only worth keeping if they chain directly into your bonus card or end-of-round goal.

Your forest habitat produces food. Start with at least 2 forest birds that give extra food dice rerolls or cached food. A strong food engine in rounds 1-2 unlocks expensive birds in rounds 3-4 when end-of-round goals intensify.

Eggs pay the cost to play new birds. Budget 1-2 eggs per bird you expect to play per round. Grassland birds with 'lay eggs' activations are your most reliable engine. Aim for 3+ egg-laying birds by end of round 2.

End-of-round goals score 5/4/3/2/1 in 5-player (1st through 5th). Scoring tiles are the same all rounds; round 1 is special only because it has the most turns (8 vs 5 in round 4) — most opportunity to compete. If your engine aligns with a goal, pivot toward it. Tied 1st beats solo 2nd.

When another player is clearly optimizing for the same end-of-round goal, evaluate whether racing is worth it. Often better to shift to a secondary goal or focus on engine building — races reduce efficiency for both players.

When drawing birds, look for low-cost birds that fit your active habitats and bonus card. Ignore high-power birds that require a completely different food mix. A focused cheap engine beats an expensive unfocused collection.

By round 3, activate your most powerful habitat every turn if possible. Count the cumulative power of each activation — a forest with 5 birds producing 3+ food dice beats spreading actions across all habitats. Depth beats breadth.

Cached food on birds converts to 1 point each at game end. Birds that cache food as part of their activation are double-dipping: they produce food when activated and store victory points. Prioritize these in round 3-4 drafting.
strategy_stealth: Cached food = 1 pt each at end
Cache birds: food + VP in one action

strategies.strategy_detailed: If you're 3+ units behind the leader on the current goal, redirect to a competing goal or bonus card. Catching up from far behind costs 2-3 actions. Those actions are worth more toward your engine or a goal where you're already competitive.

When leading a goal, take the minimum actions needed to maintain that lead and redirect surplus actions to bonus card completion or bird placement. Overinvesting in a goal you'll win anyway wastes efficiency.
strategy_stealth: Min actions to hold goal lead
Overinvesting in won goals wastes turns

strategies.strategy_detailed: In the final round, count exact points from each action option: bird card points + eggs on it vs. eggs laid vs. food cached vs. tucked cards. The bird with the highest point density per remaining action slot wins. Don't auto-play — calculate.
strategy_stealth: Count exact pts per action option

strategies.strategy_detailed: If your bonus card requires one more bird to complete, play that bird before any other action in the final round even if it costs extra eggs. Bonus card points are often 5-8 — worth two full turns of engine output.
strategy_stealth: Pay extra eggs to finish it

strategies.condition: Trailing by 5-10 points entering final round
strategies.strategy_detailed: If 5-10 points behind, focus on maximum-density plays: high-point birds, egg accumulation on high-capacity nests, and completing bonus cards. Don't try to catch up on end-of-round goals — they give too few points per action at this stage.
